}}

The '''Venezuela national football team''' ({{langx|es|Selección de fútbol de Venezuela}}), nicknamed '''''La Vinotinto''''' ("The Red Wine"), represents [[Venezuela]] in men's [[FIFA|international]] [[association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Venezuelan Football Federation|Federación Venezolana de Fútbol]] ({{langx|en|Venezuelan Football Federation}}), the governing body for [[football in Venezuela]]. Their nickname is a reference to the unique “red wine” team color that is used on their home jerseys. When playing at home in official games, they usually rotate between three stadiums: The [[Polideportivo Cachamay]] in [[Puerto Ordaz, Venezuela|Puerto Ordaz]], the [[Estadio José Antonio Anzoátegui]] in [[Puerto La Cruz]] and the [[Estadio Pueblo Nuevo]] in [[San Cristóbal, Táchira|San Cristóbal]], though recent [[FIFA World Cup]] qualifying campaigns saw home games in other stadiums around the country, including the [[Estadio Monumental de Maturín|Estadio Monumental]] in [[Maturín]]. Likewise, in friendly matches, they tend to rotate between the rest of the stadiums in the country.

Unlike other South American nations, and akin to some [[Caribbean]] nations, [[baseball]] is extremely popular in Venezuela, which diverts athletic talent away from football, contributing to its historic lack of success in [[CONMEBOL]] competitions. They are the only CONMEBOL team to have never qualified for the [[World Cup]]. Often Venezuela would go through entire qualification tournaments without recording a single win, but this has not happened since 1998. Until [[2011 Copa América|Copa América 2011]], their best finish at the tournament was fifth in their first entry, in [[South American Championship 1967|1967]]. It is only recently with the spread of the World Cup's popularity in nations where football was not the primary sport (such as Canada, Japan, the United States, and Australia) that the national team found incentives to increase player development and fan support. As of December 2019, Venezuela has the highest position on the FIFA World Ranking of any team that has not yet qualified for the World Cup, being ranked 25th.<ref>{{cite web |title=The FIFA/Coca-Cola World Ranking |url=https://www.fifa.com/fifa-world-ranking/men|publisher=FIFA|access-date=28 November 2018 |archive-date=15 July 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180715171048/https://www.fifa.com/fifa-world-ranking/ranking-table/men/index.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

==History==
{{More citations needed|section|date=June 2026}}

===20th century===
Venezuela did not participate in [[FIFA World Cup qualification]] until the [[1966 FIFA World Cup|1966]] qualifiers in which they were drawn with [[Uruguay national football team|Uruguay]] and [[Peru national football team|Peru]], but failed to register a point in four games. In the [[1970 FIFA World Cup|1970]] qualifiers they managed to register a point, and after withdrawing from the [[1974 World Cup|1974]] series, repeated that in the [[1978 World Cup|1978]] qualifiers. The [[1982 World Cup|1982]] qualifiers saw them register their first win, over Bolivia. They wouldn't register another World Cup qualifying win until the [[1994 World Cup|1994]] series when they defeated [[Ecuador national football team|Ecuador]]. A highlight of the [[1998 World Cup|1998]] qualifiers was goalkeeper [[Rafael Dudamel]] scoring against [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]] in a 5–2 defeat.

Despite poor results during the 1960s and 1970s, outstanding players like [[Luis Mendoza (Venezuelan footballer)|Luis Mendoza]] and [[Rafael Santana (footballer)|Rafael Santana]] achieved recognition. Venezuela also managed to qualify for the [[Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics|1980 Summer Olympics]] around this time, the first-ever major international football competition they participated in.

===Richard Páez era===
After [[José Omar Pastoriza]]'s resignation during the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)|2002 World Cup qualifying]] campaign, [[Richard Páez]] took over as head coach of the national team. In their remaining qualifiers, Venezuela achieved 4 victories in a row against [[Uruguay national football team|Uruguay]], [[Chile national football team|Chile]], [[Peru national football team|Peru]], and [[Paraguay national football team|Paraguay]]. In terms of World Cup qualifying matches, this was the first time the team won more than one game in row and away from home as well as avoided finishing in last place in their entire history.

However, the team failed to qualify for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002 World Cup]], or the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]], gaining 16 and 18 points respectively. However, the team did achieve progression to the second round of the [[2007 Copa America]] on home soil.

In November 2007, Páez resigned after disagreements with the media and supporters.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.emol.com/noticias/deportes/2007/11/26/283172/venezuela-se-quedo-sin-dt-renuncio-richard-paez.html |title=Venezuela se quedó sin DT: renunció Richard Páez|website=Emol.com|date=26 November 2007 |access-date=7 January 2020|language=es|archive-date=24 January 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210124184316/https://www.emol.com/noticias/deportes/2007/11/26/283172/venezuela-se-quedo-sin-dt-renuncio-richard-paez.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

===César Farías era===
With a new coach [[César Farías]], Venezuela national team improved their performances. At the beginning of [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)|2010 FIFA World Cup qualification]], Venezuela won its first game in the World Cup qualification against long-unbeaten Ecuador in [[Quito]] and received its first point against [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]]. Despite not reaching the final tournament, Venezuela achieved its best result in qualification. It finished this round with 22 points in 18 matches, surpassing Peru and Bolivia for eighth place in the region.

On 6 June 2008, Venezuela achieved its first-ever triumph over Brazil, defeating the ''Seleção'' 2–0 in a friendly match in [[Boston]], United States. Venezuela obtained excellent results in the [[2011 Copa América]] when they finished fourth, their highest finish in the tournament to date. With a squad composed mostly of footballers playing in Europe, they began [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)|2014 World Cup qualification]] with a historic 1–0 victory against Argentina in [[Puerto La Cruz]], beating it for the first time.

===Noel Sanvicente era===
[[File:Galiciavenezuela2.jpg|thumb|200px|Match between [[Galicia national football team|Galicia]] (in white) and Venezuela (in burgundy).]]
On 4 September 2014, [[Noel Sanvicente]] was made coach of the Venezuela national team.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/world-match-centre/news/newsid/240/651/2/index.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140731180110/http://www.fifa.com/world-match-centre/news/newsid/240/651/2/index.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=31 July 2014|title=Match Centre|publisher=FIFA|access-date=4 January 2017}}</ref> The team's first match under Sanvicente was against [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] in [[Bucheon]] on 5 September 2014, ending in a 3–1 defeat.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.goal.com/en/match/korea-republic-vs-venezuela/1802751/report|title=Korea Republic 3–1 Venezuela|website=Goal|date=9 May 2014|access-date=4 January 2017|archive-date=10 October 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010234459/http://www.goal.com/en/match/korea-republic-vs-venezuela/1802751/report|url-status=live}}</ref>

Venezuela began the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)|World Cup qualification]] campaign with a 1–0 defeat against [[Paraguay national football team|Paraguay]] at home, and would not earn their first point until their match against Peru, a 2–2 draw in [[Lima]] where Venezuela led until the last minute of stoppage time. Their match with Chile ended in a disappointing 4–1 defeat. Sanvicente announced his resignation a week later after mutual consent with the FVF. At the time of Sanvicente's departure, Venezuela was last in the qualification standings with a sole point.

===Rafael Dudamel era===
Sanvicente was replaced by former ''Vinotinto'' goalkeeper [[Rafael Dudamel]], who decided to revamp the entire national team, by injecting the team with the promising young generation of Venezuelan players that finished second at the [[2017 FIFA U-20 World Cup]] that was dubbed as the country's first-ever football Golden Generation.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.footballparadise.com/jovenes-venezuela-golden-u20-generation/|title=Jóvenes - Where Are Venezuela's Golden U20 Generation Now?|website=Football Paradise|date=11 June 2019|access-date=20 March 2020|archive-date=27 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927205804/https://www.footballparadise.com/jovenes-venezuela-golden-u20-generation/|url-status = live}}</ref> Under his management, ''La Vinotinto'' quickly improved and reached the quarterfinals in the [[Copa América Centenario]], with two 1–0 victories against [[Jamaica national football team|Jamaica]] and Uruguay and a 1–1 draw against [[Mexico national football team|Mexico]] in the group stage and then a 4–1 defeat to Argentina in the quarter-finals. In the 7th matchday of the 2018 World Cup qualifier, Venezuela lost against Colombia 2–0 in [[Barranquilla]], the first loss against ''Los Cafeteros'' since 2009. Later, on matchday 11, Venezuela won for the first time in the qualifier, 5–0 against [[Bolivia national football team|Bolivia]] in [[Maturín]] with a hat-trick from [[Josef Martínez]] and goals from [[Jacobo Kouffati]] and [[Rómulo Otero]].

On 2 January 2020, Dudamel resigned from the national team.

==Copa América history==
Venezuela first participated at the [[Copa América]] in [[1967 South American Championship|1967]], and finished fifth after defeating Bolivia 3–0 with a side containing Mendoza and Santana. The [[1975 Copa América|1975]] tournament saw Venezuela drawn in a group with Brazil and Argentina, and finished bottom with an 11–0 defeat against Argentina. In the [[1979 Copa América|1979]] edition, which would be the international swansong for Mendoza and Santana, they held a goalless drew Colombia and 1–1 against Chile. A highlight of the [[1989 Copa América|1989]] tournament was midfielder [[Carlos Maldonado (footballer)|Carlos Maldonado]]'s four goals. In the [[1993 Copa América|1993]] series, Venezuela drew with Uruguay and the United States.

The team's overall Copa América record has been relatively poor (goal difference 33–145 before the 2011 Copa América), but the "''Auge Vinotinto''" (Vinotinto Rise) period in the early 2000s (decade) brought increased attention to the sport in the country, which in turn brought increased support from both government and private institutions. Said support contributed perfectly to the "''Vinotinto''" rise in quality. In 2007, during the [[2007 Copa América|Copa América held in Venezuela]], the team progressed to the quarterfinals for the first time in its history after finishing first in a group containing Peru, Bolivia, and Uruguay. Venezuela's 2–0 victory against Peru during the competition was its first Copa América victory since 1967.

===2011 Copa América===
At the [[2011 Copa América]] championship, Venezuela reached the semi-finals round for the first time by defeating Chile in the quarter-final, 2–1. Despite their commanding presence against Paraguay in their semifinal, Venezuela was unable to convert their chances into goals. They would eventually lose 5–3 to Paraguay in a penalty shootout after remaining scoreless in normal and extra time. Venezuela and Peru played for third place at the [[Estadio Ciudad de La Plata]], where Venezuela would suffer their biggest loss of the tournament, losing 4–1 against Peru and falling into fourth place overall. Nonetheless, it was their best-ever finish at the competition.

==Team image==
{{Commons|Venezuela national football team kits}}
Venezuela made its international debut in the [[Central American and Caribbean Games]] held in [[Panama]] in 1938, wearing the ''vinotinto'' ([[burgundy (color)|burgundy]]) color. The burgundy color originated from the uniform of the Venezuelan National Guard.<ref>{{cite news |title=¿Por qué le dicen la Vinotinto a la Selección venezolana? {{!}} Goal.com |url=https://www.goal.com/es-cl/noticias/por-que-le-dicen-la-vinotinto-a-la-seleccion-venezolana/1mb611fmtkoxw17t8gqss0zuo1 |access-date=16 March 2023 |work=www.goal.com |date=18 June 2021 |language=es |archive-date=16 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230316225546/https://www.goal.com/es-cl/noticias/por-que-le-dicen-la-vinotinto-a-la-seleccion-venezolana/1mb611fmtkoxw17t8gqss0zuo1 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the [[1967 Copa América]] Venezuela also wore the [[Peñarol]] shirt v [[Chile national football team|Chile]] to avoid colors clash, as Venezuela had arrived in the [[Estadio Centenario]] (Peñarol's frequent venue) with no alternate shirts.<ref>{{cite web |author1=Redacción Aguanten Che |title=Vinotinto aurinegra |url=http://www.aguantenche.com.uy/2013/01/vinotinto-aurinegra/ |website=aguantenche.com.uy|access-date=18 January 2013|language=es|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130123224655/http://www.aguantenche.com.uy/2013/01/vinotinto-aurinegra/ |archive-date=23 January 2013}}</ref>

In 1993, a vertical band with the colors of the [[flag of Venezuela|National flag]] was added to the left side of the jersey, which changed its colors to a more traditional red tone. This lasted until 1996 when Venezuela returned to the ''vinotinto'' tone.<ref name=tienda/>

In 1998, Venezuela adopted a yellow/blue/red scheme, similar to their flag colors, by Mexican manufacturer "ABA Sports".<ref name=tienda>{{Cite web |url=https://www.latiendavenezolana.com/blogs/tienda/la-evolucion-de-la-camisa-vinotinto-desde-1938 |title=La evolución de la camisa vinotinto desde 1938 |access-date=2 September 2019 |archive-date=23 September 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200923214053/https://www.latiendavenezolana.com/blogs/tienda/la-evolucion-de-la-camisa-vinotinto-desde-1938 |url-status=dead }}</ref> The national team returned to the traditional color in 2000. It has been remaining (with few changes)<ref>[http://www.lapatilla.com/site/2014/03/04/la-vinotinto-estrenara-uniforme-en-el-amistoso-con-honduras-fotos/ La Vinotinto estrenará uniforme] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170702213138/http://www.lapatilla.com/site/2014/03/04/la-vinotinto-estrenara-uniforme-en-el-amistoso-con-honduras-fotos/ |date=2 July 2017 }} on La Patilla website</ref> as the main uniform up to present days.

===Kit providers===
Source:<ref>[http://www.meridiano.com.ve/futbol/la-vinotinto/184515/las-marcas-que-han-vestido-a-la-vinotinto.html Las marcas que han vestido a la Vinotinto] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190820205505/http://www.meridiano.com.ve/futbol/la-vinotinto/184515/las-marcas-que-han-vestido-a-la-vinotinto.html |date=20 August 2019 }} on Meridiano.com</ref>

|-

|-
| Head coach || {{flagicon|VEN}} [[Oswaldo Vizcarrondo]]
|-
| Assistant coach || {{flagicon|BRA}} [[Cleber Xavier]]
|-
| Assistant coach || {{flagicon|VEN}} [[Fernando Aristeguieta]]
|-
| Physical trainer || {{flagicon|URU}} Óscar Ortega
|-
| Goalkeeper coach || {{flagicon|COL}} Mario Marín

|}

===Coaching history===
:Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|ITA|1861}} Vittorio Godigna (1938)
*{{flagicon|PER|1884}} Sixto Soler (1944–1946)
*{{flagicon|VEN|1930}} Álvaro Cartea (1947–1948)
*{{flagicon|BRA|1889}} [[Orlando Fantoni]] (1951, 1955–1959)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Miguel Ángel Gleria]] (1951)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Rafael Franco (footballer)|Rafael Franco]] (1961–1967)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Gregorio Gómez (football manager)|Gregorio Gómez]] (1967–1969)
*{{flagicon|VEN|1930}} [[Rafael Gonzalez (football manager)|Rafael Gonzalez]] (1970-1972)
*{{flagicon|VEN|1930}} [[José Julián Hernández]] (1972)
*{{flagicon|GRE|old}} [[Dan Georgiadis]] (1972–1977)
*{{flagicon|VEN|1930}} [[Luis Mendoza (footballer, born 1945)|Luis Mendoza]] (1981, 1989)
*{{flagicon|URU}} [[Walter Roque]] (1981–1985)
*{{flagicon|VEN|1930}} [[Rafael Santana (footballer)|Rafael Santana]] (1985–1986, 1996)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} Carlos Horacio Moreno (1989)
*{{flagicon|URU}} [[Víctor Pignanelli]] (1990–1992)
*{{flagicon|FRY}} [[Ratomir Dujković]] (1992–1995)
*{{flagicon|COL}} [[Eduardo Borrero]] (1997–1998)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[José Omar Pastoriza]] (1998–2000)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[Richard Páez]] (2001–2007)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[César Farías]] (2007–2013)
*{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Manuel Plasencia]] (2014)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[Noel Sanvicente]] (2014–2016)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[Rafael Dudamel]] (2016–2020)
*{{flagicon|POR}} [[José Peseiro]] (2020–2021)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[Leonardo González (Venezuelan footballer)|Leonardo González]] (2021)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[José Pékerman]] (2021–2023)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Fernando Batista]] (2023–2025)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[Fernando Aristeguieta]] (2025)
*{{flagicon|VEN}} [[Oswaldo Vizcarrondo]] (2025, 2026–)
{{div col end}}

==Players==

===Current squad===
The following players were called up to the squad for the [[Exhibition game|friendly match]]es against [[Turkey national football team|Turkey]] and [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]] on 6 and 9 June 2026, respectively.<ref>{{cite instagram |user=selevinotinto |postid=DYp9AKBj8zA |title=📋 Convocatoria - 𝗙𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗮 𝗙𝗜𝗙𝗔 Junio 2026 |date=22 May 2026 |access-date=22 May 2026 |language=es}}</ref>

*''Caps and goals are correct as of 9 June 2026, after the match against [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]].''
*Friendlies not recognized by FIFA are not counted.

{{nat fs g start}}

{{nat fs break}}
*<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury
*<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad
*<sup>SUS</sup> Suspended
*<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew from the squad
{{nat fs end}}

==Player records==
{{updated|30 March 2026}}<ref>{{cite web |last1=Mamrud |first1=Roberto |title=Venezuela - Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/venez-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=30 March 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090330030627/http://www.rsssf.com/miscellaneous/venez-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Venezuela.''

===Most appearances===
[[File:USAvVEN 2019-06-09 - Tomás Rincón (51169629847) (cropped).jpg|thumb|190px|Midfielder [[Tomás Rincón]] is the most capped player with 143 appearances.]]
{| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center";
|-
!width=30 |Rank
!width=150 |Player
!width=50 |Caps
!width=50 |Goals
!width=100 |Career
|-
| 1 ||align=left| [[Tomás Rincón]] || 143 || 1 || 2008–2025
|-
| 2 ||align=left| [[Juan Arango]] || 129 || 23 || {{nowrap|1999–2015}}
|-
| 3 ||align=left| '''[[Salomón Rondón]]''' || 122 || 50 || 2008–present
|-
| 4 ||align=left| [[José Manuel Rey]] || 115 || 10 || 1997–2011
|-
| 5 ||align=left| '''[[Roberto Rosales]]'''|| 96 || 1 || 2007–present
|-
| 6 ||align=left| [[Jorge Alberto Rojas]] || 87 || 3 || 1999–2009
|-
| 7 ||align=left| [[Miguel Mea Vitali]] || 84 || 1 || 1999–2012
|-
| 8 ||align=left| [[Oswaldo Vizcarrondo]] || 80 || 7 || 2004–2016
|-
| 9 ||align=left| [[Gabriel Urdaneta]] || 77 || 9 || 1996–2005
|-
|10 ||align=left| [[Luis Vallenilla]] || 76 || 0 || 1996–2007
|}

===Top goalscorers===
[[File:Salomón Rondón 2021.jpg|thumb|190px|[[Salomón Rondón]] is the nation's all-time top goalscorer.]]
{| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center"
|-
!width=30 |Rank
!width=130 |Player
!width=50 |Goals
!width=50 |Caps
!width=50 |Ratio
!width=100 |Career
|-
| 1 ||align=left| '''[[Salomón Rondón]]''' || 50 || 122 || {{#expr:50/122 round 2}} || 2008–present
|-
| 2 ||align=left| [[Juan Arango]] || 23 || 129 || {{#expr:22/129 round 2}} || 1999–2015
|-
| 3 ||align=left| [[Giancarlo Maldonado]] || 22 || 65 || {{#expr:22/65 round 2}} || 2003–2011
|-
| 4 ||align=left| '''[[Josef Martínez]]''' || 15 || 70 || {{#expr:15/70 round 2}} || 2011–present
|-
| 5 ||align=left| [[Ruberth Morán]] || 14 || 63 || {{#expr:14/63 round 2}} || 1996–2007
|-
| rowspan=2| 6 ||align=left| [[Miku (footballer)|Miku]] || 11 || 50 || {{#expr:11/50 round 2}} || 2006–2015
|-
|align=left| '''[[Darwin Machís]]''' || 11 || 52 || {{#expr:11/52 round 2}} || 2011–present
|-
| rowspan=2| 8 ||align=left| [[Daniel Arismendi]] || 10 || 30 || {{#expr:10/30 round 2}} || 2006–2011
|-
|align=left| [[José Manuel Rey]] || 10 || 115 || {{#expr:10/115 round 2}} || 1997–2011
|-
| 10 ||align=left| [[Gabriel Urdaneta]] || 9 || 77 || {{#expr:9/77 round 2}} || 1996–2005
|}

===Copa América===
{{main|Venezuela at the Copa América}}

{{Color box|gold|border=darkgray}} '''Champions'''&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|silver|border=darkgray}} '''Runners-up'''&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|#cc9966|border=darkgray}} Third place&nbsp;&nbsp;{{Color box|LemonChiffon|border=darkgray}} Fourth place&nbsp;&nbsp;

==Honours==
===Regional===
* '''[[Football at the Bolivarian Games|Bolivarian Games]]'''
** {{silver2}} Silver medal (5): 1947–48{{sup|s}}, 1951, 1965, 1970, 1977
** {{bronze3}} Bronze medal (2): 1961, 1981

'''Friendly'''
* '''[[FIFA Series]]'''
**{{silver2}} '''Runner-up (1):''' [[2026 FIFA Series (men's matches)#Uzbekistan|2026 Uzbekistan]]<ref name="2026 FIFA Series result">{{cite web |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=March 30, 2026 |title=New Zealand net four as quartet win shootouts |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/series/articles/fifa-series-matchday-six-round-up |website=fifa.com |location= |publisher=[[FIFA]] |access-date=April 6, 2026}}</ref>

;Notes:
* {{sup|s}} Shared titles.
